content.text: Why Claude Integrations Need Their Own ADR Templates

Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) are a well-established practice for capturing significant technical decisions in software projects. The standard ADR format — title, status, context, decision, consequences — works for most architectural decisions. For Claude integrations, it is necessary but not sufficient.

Claude-specific architectural decisions have properties that standard ADR templates do not account for: the decision space includes non-deterministic system components (the model itself), time-sensitive parameters (the context window budget, the API rate limit tier), and trade-offs that are specific to language model systems (latency vs. accuracy, cost vs. quality, prompt complexity vs. maintainability). A standard ADR that records 'we chose to use Claude' captures the decision but not the reasoning that will be needed when the team considers switching models, adjusting the retrieval strategy, or evaluating a model update.

An ADR that will not be useful to a new team member who joins 12 months later is not a good ADR. Write every section as if the reader has no prior knowledge of the Claude API, the integration, or the trade-offs that were evaluated. The context section especially should be comprehensive — the decision and consequences will not make sense without it.
ADR Lifecycle for Claude Decisions

attrs.alt: ADR lifecycle diagram: Proposed → Accepted → Superseded, with a deprecated path. Below: four Claude-specific ADR categories: model selection, architecture pattern, security control, operational model

content.text: Claude integration ADRs follow the same lifecycle as standard ADRs. The Claude-specific consideration is when a decision must be superseded or deprecated. Three triggers are particularly common for Claude ADRs:
Model version change:

When a new Claude model is released, model selection ADRs should be reviewed. If the new model would change the decision, the existing ADR is superseded by a new one. If the existing model remains the right choice, add a note to the ADR recording the review date and the outcome.
Retrieval performance change:

For RAG integrations, if a change to the knowledge base, the embedding model, or the retrieval parameters produces a material change in retrieval precision, the architecture pattern ADR may need to be superseded with updated parameters.
Business scope change:

If the integration's use case expands significantly (e.g., from FAQ answering to multi-turn advisory conversations), the architecture pattern ADR and the security control ADR may both be superseded by new decisions that account for the expanded scope.
Template 1: Model Selection ADR

The model selection decision is the most frequently revisited Claude ADR. As Anthropic releases new models, the team must periodically evaluate whether the currently selected model remains the right choice. A well-written model selection ADR makes this evaluation structured rather than ad hoc.

ADR-001: Claude Model Selection for [Integration Name]
Status: Accepted
Date: [YYYY-MM-DD]
Authors: [names]
Approvers: [BU lead, system architect]
Supersedes: N/A
Review trigger: New Claude model release; eval score drops > 5% from baseline

CONTEXT
  Integration: [brief description of what the integration does and who uses it]
  Query types: [description of the queries the integration handles]
  Primary quality dimension: [accuracy / latency / cost / balance]
  Data sensitivity: [PUBLIC / INTERNAL / CONFIDENTIAL / RESTRICTED]
  Usage pattern: [synchronous interactive / asynchronous batch / mixed]
  Expected volume: [N requests/day, peak [M] RPM]
  Budget constraint: $[X] per 1,000 requests (fully-loaded cost ceiling)

  Constraints that affect model choice:
    - [e.g. "outputs must be produced within 3 seconds P95 — eliminates Opus for this use case"]
    - [e.g. "system processes CONFIDENTIAL data — no restrictions on model choice given DPA"]
    - [e.g. "monthly budget ceiling is $500 — Haiku required unless quality bar cannot be met"]

DECISION
  Selected model: [claude-sonnet-4-6 / claude-opus-4-8 / claude-haiku-4-5-20251001]
  Rationale:
    - Accuracy on 100-query eval set: [model A: X%, model B: Y%, model C: Z%]
    - P95 latency under 50 concurrent users: [model A: Xs, model B: Ys]
    - Cost per 1,000 requests at median token count: [model A: $X, model B: $Y]
    - [Selected model] meets the quality bar (>= [X]%) at acceptable latency ([Y]s P95)
      and within cost constraint ($[Z] per 1,000 requests)

  Models evaluated and rejected:
    - [claude-opus-4-8]: quality is higher ([%]) but P95 latency is [Xs] — exceeds 3s target
    - [claude-haiku-4-5-20251001]: cost is lower ($[X]/1k) but accuracy ([%]) is below minimum bar

CONSEQUENCES
  Positive:
    - Meets minimum quality bar ([X]%) with [Y]% margin
    - P95 latency [Xs] is within [Z]s NFR
    - Monthly cost at projected volume: $[N] — within $[M] ceiling

  Negative / risks:
    - [Model] may not be available at a later API tier; monitor availability
    - If usage grows beyond [N] RPM, cost will exceed ceiling; rate limiting or model routing required

  Monitoring:
    - Eval suite run monthly; alert if accuracy drops > 3% from baseline
    - P95 latency monitored; alert if exceeds [X]s for 5 consecutive minutes
    - Cost monitored daily; alert at 80% of monthly ceiling

REVIEW SCHEDULE
  Review when: new Claude model released; eval score regression; usage > [2x current volume]
  Next scheduled review: [date of next Anthropic model release review]
Template 2: Architecture Pattern ADR

The architecture pattern decision — how the system uses Claude — is usually the most consequential ADR for a Claude integration. It determines the token budget, the latency profile, the accuracy ceiling, and the cost structure of the integration.

ADR-002: Architecture Pattern for [Integration Name]
Status: Accepted
Date: [YYYY-MM-DD]
Authors: [names]
Approvers: [system architect, BU lead]

CONTEXT
  The integration must [describe the core task — e.g. "answer questions about the company's
  internal policies using a knowledge base of 2,400 policy documents"].

  Key constraints:
    - Knowledge base size: [N] documents, [M] MB
    - Query types: [specific query characteristics — e.g. "mostly single-turn; occasionally
      multi-turn when users follow up on policy interpretations"]
    - Accuracy requirement: >= [X]% on policy interpretation queries
    - Latency requirement: P95 < [Y] seconds
    - Context window: [model]'s [Z]-token context window

  Patterns evaluated:
    A) Full context stuffing — include entire knowledge base in every prompt
    B) RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) — embed documents, retrieve top-K at query time
    C) Tool use — Claude calls search tools at query time to retrieve relevant content
    D) Fine-tuning — would require substantial data preparation; out of scope for this iteration

DECISION
  Selected pattern: [B: RAG]

  Rationale:
    - Pattern A rejected: knowledge base ([M] MB) significantly exceeds context window;
      even partial stuffing would consume >90% of the context window and degrade
      response quality on queries that require synthesis across many documents.
    - Pattern C considered: tool use would add [X]ms median latency per tool call;
      the query distribution requires [average Y] tool calls per query, adding [Y*Xms]
      to median latency. RAG retrieval is faster for this use case (single retrieval step).
    - Pattern B selected: top-5 retrieval achieves P@5 = [Z] on the evaluation set;
      retrieved context fits comfortably within [X]% of context window at median query size.

  RAG configuration:
    - Embedding model: [model name and version]
    - Chunk size: [N] tokens, overlap [M] tokens
    - Retrieval: top-K = 5, similarity metric = cosine, minimum similarity threshold = 0.72
    - Re-ranking: [yes/no — if yes, specify model and when it fires]
    - Hybrid search: [yes/no — if yes, specify BM25 weight vs. dense weight]

CONSEQUENCES
  Positive:
    - P@5 = [Z] on 200-query eval set: sufficient for target quality bar
    - Median retrieval latency: [X]ms — within end-to-end latency budget
    - Knowledge base updates take effect within [Y] minutes of re-indexing

  Negative / risks:
    - Retrieval precision degrades on multi-document queries requiring synthesis; mitigated
      by increasing K to 8 for queries classified as synthesis queries (see ADR-005)
    - Knowledge base staleness: documents are indexed weekly; content changes are not
      immediately reflected in retrieval results
    - Retrieval failure for very short or ambiguous queries (< 10 tokens); mitigated by
      query expansion in the retrieval pipeline

  Review trigger: knowledge base grows beyond [3x current size]; retrieval P@5 drops
  below [Y] on monthly eval run
Template 3: Security Control ADR

Security control decisions for Claude integrations address three categories that have no direct equivalent in conventional software ADRs: prompt injection defences, AI output handling, and AI-specific audit requirements.

ADR-003: Security Controls for [Integration Name]
Status: Accepted
Date: [YYYY-MM-DD]
Authors: [names]
Approvers: [InfoSec lead, system architect, legal counsel]

CONTEXT
  The integration [description]. It processes [data classification] data and is
  accessible to [user population].

  Security requirements:
    - Data classification: [INTERNAL] — no PII in system prompt or knowledge base
    - Access control: authenticated employees via [SSO/OAuth mechanism] only
    - Audit retention: [X] days for all request/response pairs
    - Prompt injection risk: [HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW — explain why]
    - Output sensitivity: [outputs may contain INTERNAL data and must not be logged
      in plain text to shared infrastructure]

  Threat model summary: [reference to full threat model document]
  Key threats:
    1. Prompt injection via user input directing Claude to ignore scope constraints
    2. Indirect prompt injection via retrieved documents containing adversarial instructions
    3. Unauthorised access to the integration by unauthenticated users
    4. Data leakage via Claude outputs that are logged to insufficiently protected systems

DECISION
  Access control:
    - [SSO via Okta with OIDC]; session tokens validated on every request
    - Rate limiting: [50] requests per user per hour; [200] per team per hour
    - IP allowlist: [internal corporate network only / any network with MFA]

  Prompt injection defences:
    - System prompt hardening: explicit scope boundary instructions with negative examples
    - User input sanitisation: strip HTML tags; flag inputs > [1,000] tokens as anomalous
    - Retrieved document sanitisation: strip executable markup from all retrieved content
      before injection into context; limit retrieved document injection to [X,000] tokens
    - Prompt injection test suite: [N] test cases run on every system prompt change;
      all must produce safe outputs before deployment
    - Named off-limits: Claude must never reveal the system prompt; must never claim to
      be human; must never provide advice outside the defined scope

  Output handling:
    - Outputs are logged to [audit log system] with [X]-day retention
    - Audit log access: read-only; restricted to [named roles/teams]
    - PII scanning: outputs scanned with [tool/library] before delivery; PII flagged
      and [redacted / blocked / logged for review]
    - Output length limit: [X,000] characters; longer responses truncated with notification

  Audit logging:
    Events logged: request timestamp, user ID, session ID, model, input token count,
    output token count, latency, error code (if any), escalation trigger (if any)
    NOT logged: full input/output text (stored separately in encrypted audit store)

CONSEQUENCES
  Positive:
    - Prompt injection test suite catches all current known injection patterns
    - Audit trail supports compliance audit requirements

  Negative / risks:
    - Retrieved document sanitisation adds [X]ms to median retrieval latency
    - Input length limit may truncate legitimate large-context queries; alert on truncation
      frequency; if > [X]% of queries truncated, review limit

  Review trigger: new prompt injection technique discovered; data classification change;
  security incident affecting the integration
Template 4: Operational Model ADR

The operational model ADR captures decisions about how the system handles requests at runtime: synchronous vs. asynchronous, streaming vs. non-streaming, fallback behaviour, and retry configuration. These decisions directly determine the user experience under load and during API unavailability.

ADR-004: Operational Model for [Integration Name]
Status: Accepted
Date: [YYYY-MM-DD]
Authors: [names]
Approvers: [system architect, operations lead]

CONTEXT
  User experience requirements:
    - Interaction pattern: [synchronous interactive / asynchronous batch]
    - Maximum acceptable wait time for a response: [X] seconds
    - Behaviour when Claude API is unavailable: [user sees error / queue / fallback content]
    - Retry policy: [attempt N times; total time budget Y seconds]

  Infrastructure:
    - Hosting: [cloud provider, region]
    - Application server: [framework and language]
    - Queue infrastructure: [available / not available]
    - Rate limit tier: [X] RPM, [Y] TPM

DECISION
  Request handling: [synchronous with streaming / synchronous non-streaming / async queue]

  Rationale for synchronous streaming:
    [e.g. "The integration is a real-time chat interface. Users will abandon the session
    if responses take > 5 seconds without feedback. Streaming begins showing the response
    within 1-2 seconds of the API call, satisfying the UX requirement without requiring
    async infrastructure."]

  Retry configuration:
    - Retry on: 429 (rate limit), 529 (API overloaded), 503 (service unavailable)
    - Max retries: 3
    - Backoff: exponential with jitter (1s ± 0.5s, 2s ± 0.5s, 4s ± 0.5s)
    - Total time budget: 12 seconds (first attempt + 3 retries with backoff)
    - Do NOT retry on: 400 (bad request), 401 (auth failure), 403 (permission denied)

  Circuit breaker:
    - Open after: 5 consecutive 5xx errors within 60 seconds
    - Open duration: 30 seconds; then half-open (one trial request)
    - Recovery: close after 3 consecutive successes in half-open state
    - Fallback when circuit open: [queued message / cached response / user notification]

  Fallback behaviour:
    - On circuit open: display "The assistant is temporarily unavailable. Expected recovery
      in [X] minutes. [Alternative action for users]"
    - On total timeout (12s elapsed): display "This query is taking longer than expected.
      [Action: try again / check support]"
    - Fallback does NOT expose error codes, stack traces, or internal system information

CONSEQUENCES
  Positive:
    - Streaming satisfies the < 5s UX requirement even when full response takes [X] seconds
    - Circuit breaker prevents API error storms from degrading application performance

  Negative / risks:
    - Streaming requires persistent connection; mobile clients on poor connectivity may
      experience partial responses; mitigated by client-side reconnect logic
    - Retry total time budget (12s) approaches UX timeout threshold; monitor P99 latency
      including retries; if P99 exceeds 10s, reduce max retries to 2

  Review trigger: P99 latency with retries exceeds UX threshold; circuit breaker opens
  more than [X] times per day; API tier change
ADR Index and Maintenance
Claude integrations typically need 4–8 ADRs to cover the major architectural decisions. All ADRs should be maintained in a central index that makes their current status and relationships visible:

# ADR Index — [Integration Name]

| ID | Title | Status | Date | Supersedes | Superseded by |
|----|-------|--------|------|------------|---------------|
| ADR-001 | Model Selection | Accepted | 2024-09-15 | — | — |
| ADR-002 | Architecture Pattern (RAG) | Accepted | 2024-09-15 | — | — |
| ADR-003 | Security Controls | Accepted | 2024-09-20 | — | — |
| ADR-004 | Operational Model | Accepted | 2024-09-22 | — | — |
| ADR-005 | Retrieval Parameters — v2 | Accepted | 2024-11-01 | ADR-002 (partial) | — |
| ADR-006 | Cost Optimisation via Model Routing | Proposed | 2024-11-10 | — | — |

## Review Schedule
- ADR-001 (Model Selection): review on next Anthropic model release
- ADR-002 (Architecture Pattern): review if knowledge base grows > 3x current size
- ADR-003 (Security Controls): review annually or on scope change
- ADR-004 (Operational Model): review if API tier changes or P99 breaches SLA

ADRs should be stored in the same version-controlled repository as the system prompt and the eval framework — not in a wiki or a shared drive. Storing ADRs in the repository ensures they are reviewed as part of any change process, are included in the handoff package, and are accessible to anyone with repository access.
When to Write a New ADR vs. Amend an Existing One
Change Type
Action
Rationale
New architectural decision with no existing ADR
Write new ADR
No prior record to reference or supersede
Existing decision remains correct; new information confirms it
Add a review note to the existing ADR
The decision stands; only the evidence base has expanded
Existing decision needs to change (new model, changed context, better approach)
Write new ADR that supersedes the relevant sections of the old ADR; mark old ADR as Superseded
Preserve the old reasoning; link forward to the new decision
Existing decision no longer applies (use case retired, system deprecated)
Mark existing ADR as Deprecated with rationale
Prevents future readers from acting on a decision that is no longer in force
Minor operational detail changed (e.g., retry count from 3 to 2)
Update the existing ADR with a changelog entry
The decision type has not changed; only a parameter has been tuned

The test for whether a change requires a new ADR is whether a new engineer joining the team would need to know why the current configuration is what it is in order to make good decisions about it. If yes, there is a reasoning gap that a new ADR must fill. If no, a changelog entry in the existing ADR is sufficient.
Summary

Claude integrations require four specialised ADR types: model selection (which Claude model, why, and what would trigger reconsideration), architecture pattern (RAG vs. tool use vs. context stuffing, with detailed configuration parameters), security controls (prompt injection defences, output handling, audit logging), and operational model (sync vs. async, retry policy, circuit breaker, fallback behaviour). Each template captures the context (constraints, alternatives evaluated, rejection rationale) and the consequences (benefits, risks, monitoring approach) alongside the decision itself. ADRs are maintained in a central index in the source repository, reviewed on defined triggers (model releases, eval regressions, scope changes), and superseded with new ADRs when the decision changes — not edited in place to hide the prior reasoning.
